coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging carbon-based nanomaterials characterized by exceptional properties, including strong and stable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, high chemical stability, excellent water solubility, and outstanding biocompatibility. These features make CQDs highly promising for diverse applications in both biomedical and agricultural fields. Notably, the physiochemical properties of CQDs can be finely tuned through various synthesis strategies and the selection of appropriate carbon precursors. This review begins by presenting the fundamental structure and optical properties of CQDs, followed by an overview of their synthetic approaches and precursor types. It then explores the wide-ranging applications of CQDs in agriculture—such as promoting plant growth, enhancing stress resistance, and detecting metal ions and environmental pollutants—as well as their biomedical uses, including bioimaging, drug delivery, gene delivery, biosensors, and photodynamic therapy are elaborated. The review concludes by emphasizing the immense potential of CQDs to transform future biomedical innovations and sustainable agricultural practices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the two main synthetic routes for carbon quantum dots (CQDs): top-down and bottom-up. The top-down approach involves the breaking down of bulk carbon structures into nanoscale CQDs via methods such as ultrasonication, electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, and arc discharge. In contrast, the bottom-up approach builds CQDs from molecular precursors using techniques such as hydrothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted reactions, microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis (MAHS), combustion, and templating. Each section of the figure represents a specific technique and its schematic depiction, highlighting the diversity of available fabrication strategies.

Graphical representation of heteroatom doping strategies in carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The left section shows single-atom doping with boron (B), fluorine (F), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P), while the right section illustrates co-doping with combinations of these atoms. The bottom panel displays common surface functional groups (e.g., –OH, –NH₂, –COOH, –CO), which are influenced by the type of dopant used. Heteroatom doping not only tailors the electronic structure of CQDs but also modulates their surface chemistry by introducing or enhancing specific functional groups.

Excitation (black curve) and emission (red curve) spectra of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), demonstrating a typical photoluminescence response with maximum excitation around 350 nm and emission centered near 460 nm. This characteristic optical behavior is attributed to surface states and quantum confinement effects within the CQDs.

Representative green and chemical precursors used for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), including natural sources (e.g., plant extracts, bacteria, food waste, and plastics) and chemical compounds (e.g., citric acid, glucose, and activated carbon).

Representative top-down synthesis methods for carbon quantum dots (CQDs): (A) Laser ablation—a pulsed laser beam is focused onto a carbon target immersed in a liquid medium, resulting in plasma formation at the focal point. The high-energy interaction leads to carbon atom fragmentation and subsequent CQD formation through photothermal and photochemical reactions. (B) Arc discharge— depicts the role of the arc-generated bubble as a confined microreactor where carbon nanostructures are formed. It also highlights the inherent phase separation between the carbonaceous materials and the main nanostructures formed within each phase. Adapted with permission from Chao-Mujica et al. (2021). (C) Electrochemical method—CQDs are produced via electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes in a suitable electrolyte, typically using a two- or three-electrode setup. The resulting carbon nanoparticles are collected by subsequent sonication and filtration, yielding a fluorescent CQD solution. (D) Ultrasonic treatment—high-frequency ultrasonic waves induce acoustic cavitation in a solution containing carbon precursors. The growth and collapse of microbubbles create localized hotspots with extreme temperature and pressure, facilitating the breakdown of precursors and the formation of CQDs.

Schematic representation of bottom-up synthesis routes for carbon quantum dots (CQDs): (A) Hydrothermal method—natural biomass-derived precursors (e.g., fruit extracts) are sealed in a high-pressure autoclave and heated under controlled temperature conditions. The hydrothermal carbonization process facilitates the breakdown and carbonization of organic molecules, leading to the formation of CQDs. The resulting solution exhibits strong photoluminescence under UV light, indicating successful synthesis. (B) Microwave-assisted synthesis—the carbon-based precursor is directly exposed to microwave irradiation. Rapid localized heating leads to carbonization, followed by sonication, filtration, centrifugation, and syringe filtration to obtain purified CQDs. (C) Microwave-assisted hydrothermal method—the precursor solution is sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to microwave irradiation. After a short reaction time (typically 10–15 minutes), the resulting mixture is cooled, and CQDs are isolated through filtration and centrifugation.

Schematic representation of various bottom-up synthesis strategies for carbon quantum dots (CQDs): (A) Combustion method for N/S co-doped CQDs—a precursor solution containing nitrogen and sulfur sources is first dried and then subjected to high-temperature combustion in air, producing a carbonaceous ash. The ash is subsequently dispersed in water, and the CQDs are extracted via filtration and dialysis. This approach enables the incorporation of heteroatoms (N, S) into the CQD structure, enhancing their optical and electronic properties. (B) Soft–hard template method—this strategy involves the self-assembly of a composite solution containing Pluronic P123 (soft template), ordered mesoporous silica (hard template), and organic carbon sources such as 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene (TMB) or diaminebenzene (DAB). After pyrolysis and removal of the templates, surface passivation is carried out. Adapted with permission from Yang et al# (2013). TEOS: tetraethyl orthosilicate.

Dual PET/FRET mechanisms in carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for ultrasensitive biosensing. (1) PET-based detection: Amine-functionalized CQDs enable Hg²⁺ sensing via fluorescence quenching (DL: 0.1 nM). (2) FRET-based detection: Antibody-conjugated CQDs achieve label-free PSA quantification through fluorescence modulation. Both mechanisms leverage CQDs’ tunable photoluminescence and biorecognition elements for selective analyte detection in biomedical and environmental systems. Abbreviations: CQDs: Carbon Quantum Dots; FRET: Förster Resonance Energy Transfer; PET: Photoinduced Electron Transfer; PSA: Prostate-Specific Antigen.

Nanomedicine applications of engineered carbon quantum dots (CQDs): Biomedical advancements (bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery) and delivery of therapeutic genetic tools (e.g., siRNA, CRISPR) to targeted cells. Light-induced therapeutic modalities: Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) and Photothermal Therapy (PTT) for precision cancer treatment.

Illustration of the dual application strategies of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in plants, showing their foliar and root delivery routes. The schematic summarizes key physiological and environmental benefits of CQDs, including enhanced stress resilience, nutrient uptake, and interaction with soil microbiota. The integration of CQDs into agricultural systems offers a promising avenue for sustainable crop improvement under both biotic and abiotic stress conditions.
Representative synthesis methods of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), precursors, quantum yield, and their merits and demerits.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging carbon-based nanomaterials characterized by exceptional properties, including strong and stable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, high chemical stability, excellent water solubility, and outstanding biocompatibility. These features make CQDs highly promising for diverse applications in both biomedical and agricultural fields. Notably, the physiochemical properties of CQDs can be finely tuned through various synthesis strategies and the selection of appropriate carbon precursors. This review begins by presenting the fundamental structure and optical properties of CQDs, followed by an overview of their synthetic approaches and precursor types. It then explores the wide-ranging applications of CQDs in agriculture—such as promoting plant growth, enhancing stress resistance, and detecting metal ions and environmental pollutants—as well as their biomedical uses, including bioimaging, drug delivery, gene delivery, biosensors, and photodynamic therapy are elaborated. The review concludes by emphasizing the immense potential of CQDs to transform future biomedical innovations and sustainable agricultural practices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon-based nanomaterials—such as graphene, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs)—have attracted considerable attention due to their distinctive physicochemical properties (Lin et al., 2018; Magesh et al., 2022; Rao et al., 2018). However, pristine forms of fullerenes, graphene, and carbon nanotubes generally exhibit low aqueous solubility, which restricts their use in fluorescence-based applications. In contrast, CQDs and many surface-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) show excellent water solubility and stable fluorescence, making them more favorable in biomedical and sensing applications (Kumar et al., 2022; Dong et al., 2024; Jan et al., 2024). The CQDs not only exhibit interesting physicochemical properties such as strong photoluminescence and excellent stability, but also offer unique advantages including high biocompatibility and facile, low-cost synthesis routes (Guan et al., 2023). These zero-dimensional nanomaterials, typically below 10 nm in size, display optical properties that are highly tunable based on their synthesis methods and surface chemistry (Cui et al., 2021).

The characteristics of fluorescent CQDs depend on three main factors including the quantum confinement effect, and the surface, and molecular states (El-Shafey, 2021). These can be effectively tuned by selecting appropriate carbon precursors and synthesis techniques. Generally, CQDs possess various functional moieties on their surface, such as –COOH, –NH₂, and –OH, which contribute to their excellent aqueous solubility, tunable surface charge, and high biocompatibility. These groups play a critical role in modulating the optical properties of CQDs; for example, –COOH groups can enhance electron-withdrawing effects that influence fluorescence efficiency, while –NH₂ groups may shift emission wavelengths through electron-donating interactions (Hu, 2016). Furthermore, these moieties determine the surface charge of CQDs: –COOH groups typically result in a negative surface charge, whereas –NH₂ groups impart a positive charge, affecting colloidal stability and electrostatic interactions with biomolecules (Privitera et al., 2016). Such surface characteristics strongly influence biological behaviors, including binding to proteins and nucleic acids and promoting cellular uptake, which are vital for applications in biosensing and drug delivery (Karakoti et al., 2015). Additionally, surface passivation and heteroatom doping (e.g., with nitrogen, boron, or sulfur) during synthesis can further tune quantum yield (QY), photoluminescence, and other physicochemical properties by altering the density and distribution of these functional groups (Ghosh et al., 2022; Elugoke et al., 2024).

These nanomaterials can be synthesized via a variety of chemical and physical techniques, which are generally classified into top-down and bottom-up categories depending on the precursor type and energy input (Guan et al., 2023; Das et al., 2024). These main synthetic routes were illustration in Fig. 1. Among them, bottom-up methods are generally preferable due to their cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness, and versatility. However, these methods often face challenges in achieving precise control over particle size. In contrast, top-down techniques are typically associated with drawbacks such as extended processing times, harsh reaction conditions, and high production costs (Sharma and Das, 2019; El-Shabasy et al., 2021).

Historically, the evolution process of CQDs can be categorized into three main stages ( Fig. 2): (i) the discovery and early developing stages (2004–2011), which included initial reports of CQDs, primarily using top-down approaches; (ii) the advanced developing stage (since 2011), in which bottom-up synthesis methods gained prominence, offering greater control over CQDs characteristics; (iii) focus on sustainability and scalability (recent years). This latest phase involves the use of biomass-derived precursors and eco-friendly solvents, making CQDs production more practical for widespread industrial and environmental applications (Cai et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021).

To date, both green and chemical precursors have been extensively utilized for the synthesis of highly fluorescent CQDs. Nevertheless, many studies prioritize resource efficiency without fully considering the environmental impact and high costs associated with certain chemical precursors (Omar et al., 2022; Dong et al., 2024). In contrast, green precursors—sourced from biomass or waste materials—are environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and low in toxicity, making them highly suitable for scalable and sustainable CQDs production. The use of agricultural or industrial waste to generate value-added CQDs also supports green economy initiatives and solid waste management strategies (​Wang et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2019). Furthermore, green-derived CQDs are often rich in nitrogen and carbon—elements essential for plant development—and can act as effective growth promoters. These materials are also naturally enriched with heteroatoms that enhance their functional properties, such as scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS), thereby improving plant resistance to environmental stress. Additionally, green CQDs have demonstrated high sensitivity in detecting heavy metals and other emerging pollutants, underscoring their potential in environmental monitoring (Wu and Li, 2022; Dong et al., 2024; Mirseyed et al., 2024).

These nanomaterials hold great promise in a range of biomedical and environmental applications, including gene and drug delivery, bioimaging, organic solar cells, and electrochemical and optical biosensing, particularly in food safety analysis. These wide-ranging uses stem from their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and tunable surface chemistry (Maiti et al., 2019; Campuzano et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Pourmadadi et al., 2023; Raeispour et al., 2024; Yazdani et al., 2022; Mohammadi et al., 2020). In addition, CQDs exhibit desirable properties such as high surface area, physicochemical stability, electroactivity, tunable conductivity, and superior optical features, making them ideal candidates for next-generation biosensors (Bagheri et al., 2017; Behboudi et al., 2022; Mahmoudi et al., 2023; Rahmandoust et al., 2023; Taati Yengejeh et al., 2021; Zoghi and Rahmandoust, 2022). To date, various antibacterial and anticancer drugs have also been achieved using varied modified CQDs (Mahani et al., 2021; Das et al., 2024; Mirseyed et al., 2024). Notably, positively charged CQDs can disrupt bacterial membranes, which are typically negatively charged, offering a novel antibacterial strategy (Nguyen et al., 2024). Additionally, researchers have highlighted the potential of CQDs with anti-inflammatory and lubricating properties in developing nanomaterials for treating rheumatoid arthritis (Qiang et al., 2023). Their excellent adsorption capacity—attributed to their active surface, porous architecture, and abundance of functional groups—enables CQDs to capture gases, toxic substances, metal ions, and organic pollutants. This feature is particularly valuable in photocatalysis, environmental remediation, and targeted drug delivery. Furthermore, CQDs have proven highly effective in removing organic contaminants from water, such as pesticides, antibiotics, and dyes (Liu et al., 2020; Kong et al., 2024).

This review is organized into several sections. It begins by outlining the structural and optical properties of CQDs, followed by an overview of their synthesis methods and precursor types. Subsequently, the cutting-edge applications of CQDs are discussed and classified into two main categories: environmental/agricultural systems and biomedical applications. Finally, the review concludes with insights into future research directions and potential developments in the field of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical properties of CQDs‎

ce:section.ce:para: Comprehensive structural characterization of CQDs is essential to understand their morphology, size, crystallinity, surface chemistry, and elemental composition, which are all crucial for tuning their optical, electrical, and catalytic properties. Recent advances have enabled a more detailed and integrated approach to these analyses.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and atomic force microscopy (AFM) remain the most commonly used tools to analyze the morphology, size distribution, and lattice fringes of CQDs. The typical lattice spacing of graphitic CQDs is ∼0.21–0.24 nm, corresponding to the (100) or (002) planes of graphite. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) is also applied for surface morphology, although with limited resolution for nanoparticles smaller than 5 nm (Li et al., 2012; Zheng et al., 2015; Yadav et al., 2023).

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) is widely used to estimate hydrodynamic diameter and polydispersity index (PDI) in colloidal suspensions. Moreover, zeta potential measurements from DLS instruments provide insights into colloidal stability and surface charge, which are essential for predicting biological behavior and dispersion stability (Yadav et al., 2023).

Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) in TEM and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns provide information about the degree of crystallinity. These nanomaterials typically exhibit broad XRD peaks at around 2θ = 22°–25°, indicating their amorphous or turbostratic graphitic nature. More crystalline samples show sharp peaks near 2θ = 26°, typical of the (002) planes in sp²-hybridized carbon. Selected area electron diffraction patterns often reveal diffuse rings for amorphous CQDs or discrete spots for more ordered structures (Zheng et al., 2015; Yadav et al., 2023).

Raman spectroscopy provides detailed insight into the carbon framework. The D-band (∼1350 cm–¹) and G-band (∼1580 cm–¹) represent disordered and graphitic carbon structures, respectively. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) indicates the degree of defects and graphitization. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy reveals functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, and carboxyl groups, often present on CQDs surfaces due to oxidation or precursor composition.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to analyze surface elements and bonding states, especially C1s, O1s, and N1s peaks. The presence of nitrogen and sulfur dopants or oxygen-containing groups can be confirmed and quantified. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), particularly ^1H and ^13 C NMR, helps elucidate surface chemistry and carbon hybridization, although its application is less common due to the broad signals from heterogeneous carbon structures. Elemental analysis (EA) complements these techniques by giving accurate carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen content (Zheng et al., 2015; Yadav et al., 2023).

Recent studies have focused on correlating structural characteristics with the synthetic method. For example, Yu et al. (2024) demonstrated that CQDs synthesized via microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis from citric acid and urea had a dominant graphitic core with abundant nitrogen surface functionalities, as confirmed by XPS and HRTEM. Another study by Luo et al. (2022) used synchrotron radiation XRD and XPS to reveal that high-temperature pyrolysis above 350 °C enhances the crystallinity and sp² domain size of CQDs, which is beneficial for optoelectronic performance. These advancements underscore the importance of multi-technique characterization for developing structure–property relationships in CQDs.

The optical properties of CQDs are one of their most significant features, enabling applications across imaging, sensing, and optoelectronics. These properties are primarily influenced by particle size, surface functional groups, degree of graphitization, and heteroatom doping. Typically, optical characterization is carried out using ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, although these techniques are discussed in detail in dedicated sections. It is generally observed that CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, a behavior attributed to the quantum confinement effect and the presence of various energy states associated with surface defects and heteroatoms.

Fluorescence in CQDs does not arise from a single mechanism. While quantum confinement within the CC and CO domains can contribute to size-tunable emission, multiple factors influence the final photoluminescence behavior. These include (i) defect states that introduce trap levels within the bandgap, (ii) molecular states originating from the carbonaceous or dopant residues, and (iii) cross-linked surface states that generate new radiative recombination pathways (Azam et al., 2021; Kong et al., 2024). This multifaceted emission mechanism provides CQDs with unique tunability that is highly desirable for multicolor imaging and sensing.

Surface defects play a pivotal role in the fluorescence emission of CQDs by introducing additional electronic states that can either enhance or quench luminescence, depending on their chemical nature and spatial distribution. Functional groups such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amine moieties commonly act as exciton trapping sites, facilitating radiative recombination processes and enabling tunable emission properties (Azam et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2024). A clear distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic fluorescence mechanisms is crucial for understanding CQDs emission behavior. Intrinsic fluorescence originates from the π-conjugated sp² domains within the carbon core, resulting in bandgap transitions associated with the quantum confinement effect. In contrast, extrinsic fluorescence arises from surface states, edge defects, and functional groups that introduce localized energy levels within the bandgap, leading to emission that is often excitation-independent (Xu et al., 2018). Furthermore, CQDs typically exhibit exceptional photostability and QY, maintaining strong fluorescence emission even under prolonged excitation or harsh environmental conditions (Zhao et al., 2008; Kong et al., 2024).

Recent studies have further emphasized the correlation between synthetic conditions and emission characteristics. For instance, doping heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, fluorine, and boron into the carbon framework can significantly modify the electronic structure of CQDs, enhancing their optical performance. Heteroatom doping introduces localized energy levels and modulates charge carrier dynamics, enabling higher quantum yields (QYs) and broader emission tunability. Moreover, co-doping strategies have emerged to further optimize emission intensity and stability through synergistic effects on surface chemistry and electronic transitions ( Fig. 3) (Kandasamy, 2019; Tian et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2022).

The absorbance profile of CQDs is closely associated with their intrinsic carbon core structure, surface chemistry, and heteroatom doping, all of which are influenced by the synthesis route and environmental conditions such as solvent polarity and pH (Yadav et al., 2023). Despite variations in size and surface functionalities, most CQDs exhibit a characteristic UV–vis absorption spectrum with two prominent regions: a strong peak between 200–260 nm attributed to π–π* transitions of the aromatic CC bonds within the sp²-hybridized domains, and a broader shoulder or peak between 280–350 nm corresponding to n–π* transitions from lone pairs on surface groups like –OH, –COOH, and CO (Azam et al., 2021; Magesh et al., 2022).

In some cases, additional absorption bands may appear in the visible region (400–600 nm), especially in heteroatom-doped CQDs or those with extensive surface oxidation. These bands are usually attributed to surface state transitions and the formation of mid-gap energy levels caused by functional groups or dopants (e.g., N, S, B) (Zhao et al., 2023). For instance, nitrogen-doped CQDs have shown red-shifted absorption due to the incorporation of pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen, which enhances the electronic conjugation and introduces new electronic transitions (Han et al., 2024).

Moreover, recent studies have demonstrated that by tailoring the degree of conjugation and introducing defects through controlled synthesis conditions, it is possible to engineer the optical bandgap and achieve visible-light-responsive CQDs suitable for solar energy harvesting and bioimaging (Zhang et al., 2024). Thus, UV–vis spectroscopy not only serves as a primary tool to probe the optical transitions but also helps assess the electronic structure and potential applications of CQDs.

Photoluminescence (PL) is a fundamental optical property of CQDs, in which the material absorbs photons and subsequently re-emits light of a longer wavelength. This process is generally classified into fluorescence and phosphorescence, with fluorescence being the dominant type in CQDs due to its rapid decay when the excitation source is removed (Yadav et al., 2023). One of the most fascinating characteristics of CQDs is their tunable PL emission, which spans across a broad spectral range from ultraviolet (UV) to visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions. This wavelength-dependent emission is typically characterized by a Stokes shift, conforming to a down-conversion mechanism (Guan et al., 2023).

The PL behavior of CQDs is highly sensitive to their size, surface chemistry, and synthesis method. Several studies have attributed the origin of CQDs’ fluorescence to two major mechanisms. The first involves π–π* transitions within sp²-hybridized carbon domains, which act as emissive centers (Baker and Baker, 2010; Zhang et al., 2021). The second mechanism emphasizes the role of quantum confinement effects and surface-related states, such as surface traps, oxygen-containing functional groups, and defects introduced by heteroatom doping (Fang et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2011; Guan et al., 2023).

Recent studies have shown that modulation of functional groups in CQDs, especially those involving heteroatom doping or surface oxidation, can greatly enhance their photoluminescent and photocatalytic properties. For instance, Liu et al. demonstrated that tailored surface groups significantly improved PL efficiency by introducing mid-gap states that facilitate radiative recombination (Liu et al., 2023).

Additionally, oxygen-containing surface groups such as –OH, –COOH, and epoxy groups have been shown to create emissive surface energy levels that enable CQDs to emit in the visible or even NIR regions. By tuning the type and density of these groups, CQDs can be customized for specific emission profiles (Gokus et al., 2009; Dekaliuk et al., 2014).

Advanced synthetic strategies now allow for the generation of CQDs that can be excited under visible light and emit in the red or NIR regions—critical for biomedical imaging and phototherapy due to reduced tissue absorption and scattering in these wavelengths (An et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021). Studies have reported the successful use of CQDs in multicolor imaging, enabling the visualization of cellular structures and molecular interactions with high resolution (Pechnikova et al., 2024).

The diverse PL properties of CQDs have led to their integration in numerous applications including biosensing, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), solar energy conversion, photodynamic therapy, and environmental monitoring. Their high photostability, biocompatibility, and tunable fluorescence make them highly promising for real-time bioimaging and theranostics. Fig. 4 illustrates a typical excitation and emission spectrum of fluorescent CQDs, highlighting their broad excitation range and sharp emission peaks (Yadav et al., 2023). Understanding and fine-tuning the PL properties of CQDs remain a focal point of current research aimed at expanding their applicability in advanced optical and biomedical technologies.

Up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) is a nonlinear optical process wherein CQDs absorb two or more low-energy photons and emit a single photon of higher energy, resulting in anti-Stokes emission. This phenomenon contrasts with the more common down-conversion PL, where emitted photons have lower energy than the absorbed ones. The mechanisms underlying UCPL in CQDs include excited-state absorption (ESA), energy transfer upconversion (ETU), and photon avalanche (PA). Among these, ETU is particularly efficient and widely studied. In ETU, energy is transferred between neighboring excited states, facilitating the emission of higher-energy photons (Wang and Liu, 2009; Laptinskiy et al., 2022).

Up-conversion photoluminescence properties in CQDs are highly dependent on factors such as particle size, surface functional groups, and the presence of heteroatoms. Surface passivation and doping with elements like nitrogen or sulfur can introduce mid-gap states that enhance UCPL efficiency (Sun et al., 2006; Azam et al., 2021). Recent studies have demonstrated the application of UCPL-active CQDs in various fields. For instance, red-emissive CQDs have been integrated with TiO₂ to enhance photocatalytic activity under visible light, showcasing the potential of UCPL in environmental remediation (Khojiev et al., 2023). In biomedical imaging, UCPL-active CQDs offer advantages such as deep tissue penetration and reduced background autofluorescence when excited with near-infrared (NIR) light. This makes them suitable for in vivo imaging applications (Himmelstoß and Hirsch, 2019). Furthermore, the synthesis of CQDs from natural sources, such as cabbage, has been explored to produce biocompatible and eco-friendly UCPL materials suitable for bioimaging (Alam et al., 2015).

The emission wavelengths in UCPL are typically consistent across different excitation wavelengths, indicating that emission occurs from the lowest excited singlet state, regardless of the excitation energy (Jia et al., 2012). In summary, UCPL in CQDs presents a promising avenue for applications in photocatalysis, bioimaging, and optoelectronics. Ongoing research into the mechanisms and enhancement strategies of UCPL will further expand the utility of CQDs in advanced technological applications.

The CQDs can be synthesized from two main types of sources: chemical and green ( Fig. 5). In recent years, increasing attention has been given to green precursors derived from a wide range of natural materials, including plant parts (such as flowers, fruits, stems, seeds, leaves, vegetables), agricultural residues (e.g., wheat straw, palm kernel shell, sago waste), microbial sources (such as algal blooms, mushroom, Shewanella oneidensis, microalgae biochar), and various types of waste materials (including expired milk, tea powders, coal powder, polypropylene plastic waste) (Omar et al., 2022, Dong et al., 2024). This approach is particularly attractive because it uses renewable, non-toxic, highly biodegradable, abundant and low-cost carbon precursors, enabling the sustainable and large-scale production of high-quality CQDs.

However, despite their advantages, green synthesis methods often suffer from poor reproducibility and batch-to-batch variations, primarily due to the inconsistent chemical composition of natural feedstocks. The properties of plant-based and microbial sources can vary significantly depending on geographic origin, seasonal changes, storage conditions, and extraction techniques. These inconsistencies may affect critical CQDs characteristics such as particle size, surface functional groups, fluorescence QY, and biological activity (Hamed et al., 2024). To address these issues, implementing standardized protocols, pretreatment of raw materials, and real-time monitoring of synthesis parameters is crucial for ensuring the reproducibility and scalability of green CQDs synthesis (Varadharajan et al., 2024).

To date, numerous biomass sources have been used as excellent precursors for the synthesis of CQDs. Zhou et al. (2021) produced blue-emitting CQDs in a hydrothermal method from ethylenediamine and quinoa saponin powder. Using combustion method, Jagannathan et al. (2021) synthesized CQDs with white emissive from corn grains. Nguyen et al. (2022) prepared blue highly fluorescent CQDs from banana peels by a hydrothermal process.

Although a wide variety of green carbon sources have been employed for the low-cost production of CQDs, chemical precursors are still widely used, particularly when high QY is desired. Generally, chemical precursors fall into two categories: non-acidic materials—such as graphite oxide, polyimide, activated carbon, chitosan, glucose, 3-bromophenol, and ethanolamine—and acidic materials, including sodium citrate, citric acid, acetic acid, glutamic acid, folic acid, and boric acid (Omar et al., 2022). Among non-acidic precursors, enhanced synthesis has been reported using various aniline derivatives—such as o-phenylenediamine, 1,2,4-benzenetriamine dihydrochloride, and 1,2,4,5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride—in combination with ethanol as a solvent (Zhao et al., 2019).

Since 2012, acid-based carbon precursors have also been widely employed in the synthesis of CQDs (Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2014; Ahmed et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2021; Huo et al., 2022). Among these, citric acid has been extensively used due to its numerous advantages over other acidic sources—it is relatively inexpensive, readily available in large quantities, and has minimal environmental impact (Omar et al., 2022). Notably, CQDs synthesized using a combination of urea and sodium citrate dihydrate have achieved a remarkably high QY of up to 93 %. In this system, sodium citrate dihydrate functions as a self-assembly inducer, while urea serves as a nitrogen dopant; together, these components synergistically enhance the optical performance and QY of the resulting CQDs (Sun et al., 2020).
There are two major approaches for synthesis of CQDs, categorized as the top-down and the bottom-up methods which are explained as follows.

The top-down approach is a widely used synthesis strategy that involves breaking down larger carbon materials into nanoscale CQDs through chemical or physical methods. This technique offers several advantages, including a broad range of available precursors such as carbon nanotubes, carbon fibers, bulk carbon, bulk g-C₃N₄, graphite powder, and graphene oxide. Additionally, it is relatively straightforward and often results in high yields. However, the method also has notable limitations—such as poor control over particle size and morphology due to the random nature of material fragmentation—as well as issues related to high costs and potential impurities in the final product (Guan et al., 2023; Das et al., 2024).

Laser ablation is a physical top-down technique widely used for the synthesis of CQDs, involving the interaction of high-energy laser pulses with a carbonaceous target ( Fig. 6A). Upon laser irradiation, the localized energy absorption leads to rapid heating, reaching temperatures exceeding 900 °C and pressures above 75 kPa. These extreme conditions induce material evaporation and plasma formation, from which CQDs nucleate and grow upon cooling in the surrounding liquid environment (Hu et al., 2011; Magesh et al., 2022; Kong et al., 2024).

There are two primary configurations for laser ablation: (i) Laser fragmentation in liquid (LFL), where a suspension of carbon nanoparticles is irradiated by laser to generate smaller CQDs, and (ii) Laser ablation in liquid (LAL), where a solid carbon target is immersed in a liquid medium and ablated by pulsed laser (Luo et al., 2014; Kong et al., 2024). Both approaches allow the tuning of CQD properties by adjusting laser parameters such as wavelength, pulse energy, repetition rate, and ablation duration.

Recent advancements in laser-assisted synthesis have significantly enhanced the efficiency and quality of CQDs. For instance, Cui et al. (2020) developed a dual-beam laser ablation method using low-cost carbon fabric, achieving CQDs with a QY of 35.4 %—a substantial improvement over traditional single-beam approaches. Similarly, Ren et al. (2019) synthesized nitrogen-doped mesoporous CQDs with a QY of 32.4 % and an impressive fluorescence lifetime of 6.56 ns using pulsed laser ablation in deionized water.

Despite its advantages—such as high purity, low contamination risk, and the absence of chemical precursors—laser ablation also presents limitations: (i) Surface defects: The high-energy process may introduce oxygen-containing groups or break sp² carbon chains, affecting optical/electronic properties. (ii) Broad size distribution: The lack of uniform control during particle fragmentation can result in polydisperse CQDs. (ii) Use of organic solvents: Solvents like N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) are often used to stabilize particles post-synthesis, raising environmental concerns. (iv) Low QY: compared with bottom-up methods, traditional laser ablation often yields CQDs with lower photoluminescence efficiency (Elugoke et al., 2024).

Nonetheless, the ability to tailor laser parameters makes this technique promising for producing functionalized or doped CQDs with tunable emission properties. It is also highly adaptable for green synthesis by selecting appropriate solvents or using water-based systems.

Arc discharge is a high-energy, top-down physical method in which a bulk carbon source decomposes at the anode through an arc plasma generated between two carbon electrodes (Fig. 6B). This method utilizes direct current in an inert or reactive atmosphere, resulting in extreme localized temperatures reaching up to ∼4000 K. At these temperatures, the carbon vaporizes and condenses into various nanostructures, including CQDs, which are then deposited at the cathode region or collected from the surrounding medium (Arora and Sharma, 2014; Das et al., 2024).

One of the earliest accidental discoveries of CQDs was made by Xu et al. (2004), who observed fluorescent nanostructures while purifying carbon nanotubes using the arc discharge method. The CQDs exhibited multicolor fluorescence—yellow, orange, and blue-green—under UV excitation (365 nm), and their excellent water dispersibility was attributed to hydrophilic carboxyl groups introduced by nitric acid treatment (Zuo et al., 2016).

Recent advances have optimized this method, particularly the Submerged Arc Discharge in Water (SADW) technique. In this configuration, the electrodes are submerged in deionized water, which acts as both a dielectric medium and a quencher of the hot carbon vapor. During the process, bubble microreactors are formed near the arc plasma, serving as nucleation sites for CQDs. The post-reaction solution typically contains three fractions: precipitated materials (multiwalled carbon nanotubes), floating matter (carbon nano-onions), and the suspended phase (CQDs with traces of graphene oxide). The latter is carefully separated for downstream applications (Wang and Hu, 2014; Kong et al., 2024).

Chao-Mujica et al. (2021) successfully synthesized CQDs using this submerged arc method. The resulting CQDs displayed particle sizes of 1–5 nm with graphite-like lattice fringes (0.34–0.35 nm interlayer spacing). These nanodots exhibited dual fluorescence bands at 320–340 nm and 400–410 nm, and a QY of 16 %, making them suitable for bioimaging applications.

Further improvements were reported by Pattanayek et al. (2024), who developed a green version of the arc discharge process using aqueous urea solutions and natural carbon sources. Their method yielded CQDs with a high QY of 28 % and strong blue fluorescence. The dots showed high photostability and were successfully used for selective Au³ ⁺ detection and in vitro cell imaging.

Despite its simplicity and ability to generate high-quality CQDs, the arc discharge method suffers from some limitations, including low yield, poor size uniformity, and energy consumption, making it less favorable for large-scale production. However, ongoing innovations such as dual-electrode configurations, plasma control, and green solvent systems are promising directions for improving its scalability and sustainability.

The electrochemical method, first introduced by Zhou et al. (2007), involves the oxidative cleavage of carbon sources using electrical potential, offering a relatively simple, green, and controllable route for CQDs synthesis. In this approach, the carbon precursors—typically conductive materials like graphite rods, carbon fibers, multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), or coal-based materials—undergo electrochemical oxidation under finely controlled current and potential conditions in a selected electrolyte medium (Hou et al., 2015; Guan et al., 2023). Common electrolytes include aqueous NaOH, NaOH/ethanol mixtures, citric acid, or organic solvents such as acetonitrile containing tetrabutylammonium perchlorate.

The general process involves immersing electrodes into the electrolyte solution, where the carbon atoms at the anode oxidize and gradually disintegrate into nanoscale CQDs. The choice of electrolyte, applied voltage, current density, and electrode material strongly influence the resulting CQD size, surface functionalization, QY, and fluorescence behavior (Li et al., 2010; Kong et al., 2024). The electrochemical route is widely appreciated for its mild reaction conditions (ambient temperature and pressure), high reproducibility, tunable particle properties, environmental friendliness, and potential for scalable production (Rocco et al., 2023).

However, certain challenges persist, including the generation of surface defects, quantum confinement effects, and incomplete oxidation, which can lead to broad size distributions and lower crystallinity in the CQDs. These structural imperfections may adversely affect the fluorescence efficiency, charge transport behavior, and catalytic performance of the particles (Guan et al., 2023).

Recent studies have demonstrated the versatility of this method. Borna et al. (2021) synthesized CQDs using two graphite electrodes immersed in an ethanol/NaOH solution. After a 3-hour electrolysis followed by ultrasonication and filtration, they obtained a yellow solution containing CQDs with tunable sizes and good fluorescence stability (Fig. 6C). Barrionuevo et al. (2023) developed a green and efficient electrochemical method for synthesizing highly crystalline CQDs through the direct electrooxidation of ethanol on nickel foam electrodes. This process yielded ultra-small, graphene-like CQDs characterized by distinct sp² and sp³ domains. The synthesized CQDs exhibited strong fluorescence properties and demonstrated remarkable photocatalytic activity, achieving approximately 90 % degradation of methylene blue within the first 5 minutes of light exposure. This method not only offers a sustainable approach to CQDs synthesis but also highlights their potential applications in environmental remediation.

Overall, the electrochemical method offers a flexible platform for producing doped or surface-functionalized CQDs by tailoring precursor materials and electrolyte systems. Its moderate reaction conditions and high tunability position it as a promising technique for advanced applications. Despite its controllability and eco-friendliness, the electrochemical method may lead to structural imperfections and broad size distributions. As a simpler and more accessible physical technique, ultrasonication offers an alternative route for CQDs synthesis using milder conditions and readily available equipment.

Ultrasonication is a simple yet efficient top-down method for synthesizing CQDs through the application of high-frequency ultrasonic waves. This technique induces acoustic cavitation—rapid formation, growth, and implosive collapse of microscopic bubbles in a liquid medium—which generates localized hotspots with extremely high temperature and pressure for very short durations. These extreme conditions facilitate the breakdown of bulk carbon materials into nanoscale fragments, including CQDs (Fig. 6D) (Guan et al., 2023; Das et al., 2024).

In recent years, ultrasonication has been widely utilized due to its low cost, environmental friendliness, and ability to scale up for industrial-level production. Various carbon sources such as graphite, carbon fibers, and biochar have been successfully employed in this method. The size, morphology, and optical properties of the resulting CQDs can be tuned by modifying process parameters, including sonication power, exposure time, solvent composition, and the carbon precursor to solvent ratio (Elugoke et al., 2024).

Despite its many advantages—such as uniform energy distribution, low temperature operation, and ease of operation—ultrasonication alone may not always result in CQDs with high crystallinity or high QY. Therefore, recent studies have shown that combining ultrasonication with other methods such as hydrothermal treatment, chemical oxidation, or microwave-assisted synthesis can significantly enhance the quality and optical performance of the CQDs (Zhuo et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2014). For instance, Wang et al. (2023) demonstrated the fabrication of CQDs from graphitic carbon nitride using ultrasonic exfoliation. The resulting CQDs exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity, particularly in the efficient production of hydrogen peroxide, highlighting the potential of ultrasonication in developing functional nanomaterials.

In summary, ultrasonication is an effective and scalable method for CQDS production, especially when combined with other synthesis techniques. However, due to limitations such as incomplete oxidation and structural defects when used alone, researchers have increasingly turned to bottom-up strategies, such as hydrothermal and microwave methods, which allow for more controlled synthesis and better-quality CQDs.

Bottom-up routes involve smaller carbon molecules that are polymerized and carbonized into CQDs. These methods are economically viable and eco-friendly, but the sizes of CQDs cannot be precisely controlled in these techniques.

The hydrothermal method is one of the most widely used and environmentally friendly approaches for synthesizing CQDs, owing to its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and ability to utilize a broad variety of natural and synthetic carbon precursors. In this process, precursors such as citric acid, phenylenediamine, chitosan, urea, or biomass are dispersed in water or organic solvents and subjected to high temperature and pressure inside a sealed Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave ( Fig. 7A) (He et al., 2018; Guan et al., 2023). The elevated temperature initiates a series of carbonization, polymerization, and dehydration reactions, eventually yielding CQDs with tunable sizes and surface functionalities.

Zhang et al. (2010) first reported the synthesis of fluorescent CQDs using this method, employing L-ascorbic acid as the carbon source. Since then, the hydrothermal approach has been optimized and widely adopted due to its versatility in producing CQDs with narrow size distributions, relatively high QYs, and surface stability. For example, the use of dual sources such as citric acid (carbon) and ethylenediamine (nitrogen) has enabled the fabrication of nitrogen-doped CQDs with excellent optical properties and potential applications in biosensing (Pu et al., 2020).

A notable advantage of this method lies in its compatibility with biomass and green precursors, reducing reliance on harsh chemicals. The CQDs synthesized from banana peels (Atchudan et al., 2021) and broccoli (Arumugam & Kim, 2018) have demonstrated great photoluminescent characteristics and have been used in bioimaging and fluorescence-based sensors, respectively. Moreover, Pang et al. (2017) synthesized S,N co-doped CQDs via a one-step hydrothermal process using ethylenediamine and methionine, which exhibited good water solubility and effective Fe³ ⁺ ion detection. Despite its advantages, the hydrothermal method has some inherent limitations. It typically requires prolonged reaction times (ranging from several hours to overnight), and precise control over the final size and uniformity of CQDs may be challenging. Furthermore, the need for high-pressure vessels adds complexity to scale-up for industrial production.

Nevertheless, the method provides a favorable platform for doping with heteroatoms (e.g., N, S, B, P), thus enabling the development of CQDs with enhanced catalytic, sensing, or optical functionalities. This tunability also makes the hydrothermal route a promising strategy for the preparation of novel electrocatalysts (Elugoke et al., 2024; Kong et al., 2024).

The microwave-assisted synthesis of CQDs is gaining increasing attention due to its rapid reaction kinetics, scalability, and energy efficiency. This method uses electromagnetic radiation in the microwave range (with wavelengths between 1 mm and 1 m) to deliver uniform volumetric heating directly to the reaction medium (Fig. 7B) (Başoğlu et al., 2020; Sousa et al., 2021). The rapid internal heating disrupts precursor bonds and accelerates carbonization, yielding CQDs with desirable optical properties in a matter of minutes.

Microwave irradiation enables homogeneous heating, thereby minimizing thermal gradients and promoting the formation of uniform, high-purity CQDs with fewer surface defects. This technique also minimizes reaction time, reduces energy consumption, and eliminates the need for harsh chemicals (Tungare et al., 2020). Several studies have highlighted its potential for high-yield synthesis. For instance, Wang et al. (2015) reported a QY of 61.3 % for N,S-doped CQDs, while Zhu et al. (2021) achieved an exceptional QY of 82.7 %, although it decreased after purification steps such as phase transfer and dialysis.

This method has been employed to synthesize functionalized CQDs for a wide range of applications. Nakano et al. (2018) fabricated nitrogen-doped CQDs from citric acid for hydrogen peroxide sensing. Similarly, Shejale et al. (2021) developed N-CQDs for incorporation into dye-sensitized solar cells, enhancing their photoelectric conversion efficiency. These CQDs exhibited strong charge carrier mobility, broadband absorption, and tunable photoluminescence, making them suitable for optoelectronic and sensing applications (Magesh et al., 2022).

However, despite its advantages, the microwave method faces some limitations. It is typically suitable only for small-scale production and offers limited control over CQDs size distribution due to the direct and often uneven interaction between microwaves and reaction media. To overcome these challenges and further improve control over reaction conditions and scalability, researchers have developed microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis (MAHS) — a hybrid approach that builds upon the strengths of both microwave and hydrothermal techniques.

Microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis (MAHS) merges the benefits of conventional hydrothermal processing with the rapid and uniform heating of microwave irradiation. In this method, microwave energy is applied to heat the precursor solution within a pressurized autoclave, enabling enhanced heat transfer and accelerated reaction kinetics (Fig. 7C). This results in the production of CQDs with controlled morphology, improved crystallinity, and higher reproducibility, all within significantly reduced reaction times (Joseph and Anappara, 2016; Huang et al., 2019).

The MAHS technique has been proven highly effective for fabricating high-quality CQDs with tunable properties. For example, Zheng et al. (2017) synthesized organosilane-functionalized carbon dots with a solid-state QY of 65.8 % in just five minutes. The microwave-hydrothermal combination allows better regulation of reaction pressure and temperature, enabling fine-tuning of particle size, surface functional groups, and emission behavior (Meng et al., 2016). Additionally, the closed-system nature of this technique minimizes contamination and enhances process safety and repeatability. Compared with standalone microwave synthesis, MAHS offers improved scalability and greater structural control, making it a promising approach for industrial-scale production of CQDs (Huang et al., 2019). Nevertheless, the need for specialized reactors and optimization of microwave parameters may pose technical challenges. As such, further development of MAHS can focus on enhancing scalability, energy efficiency, and automation for broader application in biomedicine, catalysis, and energy-related devices.

The combustion method offers a straightforward and cost-effective approach for synthesizing CQDs through the oxidative decomposition of organic, carbon-rich precursors such as citric acid, glucose, urea, and various biomass-derived compounds ( Fig. 8A). In this technique, an oxidizing agent initiates the thermal degradation of the precursor, producing carbonaceous materials that subsequently form CQDs with desirable fluorescence and water solubility (Varma et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018). Owing to its operational simplicity, scalability, and eco-friendliness, the combustion method has gained traction for both laboratory and industrial applications.

The origin of this method can be traced back to the work of Liu et al. (2007), who employed oxidative acid treatment post-combustion to improve water dispersibility, enhance photoluminescence (PL) properties, and promote surface functionalization. Further advancements have demonstrated the versatility of this technique in producing doped and multicolor CQDs. For instance, Li et al. (2017) reported the synthesis of uniform graphene quantum dots (∼8.5 nm in size) with abundant surface carboxyl groups through direct combustion of citric acid. Similarly, Cheng et al. (2019) utilized willow catkin biowaste to synthesize N/S-doped CQDs via combustion, achieving consistent particle size and surface functionalization.

A remarkable advantage of the combustion method is its ability to yield multicolor emissive CQDs in a single step. Miao et al. (2018) demonstrated this by synthesizing red-, green-, and blue-emitting CQDs from urea and citric acid, achieving respective QYs of 12.9 %, 35.1 %, and 52.6 %. These CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence and potential for applications in optoelectronics and bioimaging (Magesh et al., 2022). However, the combustion method often struggles with precise control over particle size and reproducibility. To overcome these issues, more advanced methods such as the template-assisted synthesis have been developed to produce highly uniform and structurally defined CQDs.

The template method is a sophisticated strategy for synthesizing CQDs, particularly valued for its ability to control particle size, morphology, and surface characteristics with high precision. This method involves using a structural scaffold—referred to as a template—that defines the shape and size of the resulting CQDs. Typically, the process includes two main steps: (i) calcination of carbon-rich precursors in the presence of a template (such as mesoporous silica), and (ii) subsequent removal of the template through chemical etching, thus releasing the embedded CQDs (Jing et al., 2018).

The templates are generally classified as either hard templates (e.g., zeolites, silica, layered double hydroxides, or metal-organic frameworks) or soft templates (e.g., surfactants, block copolymers, and micelles). This approach offers several advantages over conventional methods, especially in preventing nanoparticle agglomeration, enhancing surface passivation, and yielding monodisperse CQDs with tunable photoluminescent and structural features (Liu et al., 2009; Ge et al., 2021). Moreover, the confined nanospace provided by the template enables the formation of CQDs with highly crystalline cores and tailored surface chemistry, which is critical for applications requiring high fluorescence stability and narrow size distributions.

For instance, Yang et al. (2013) demonstrated the successful synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots using a dual-template system comprising mesoporous silica (hard template) and Pluronic P123 (soft template), with diaminebenzene (DAB) and 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene (TMB) as carbon sources. The resulting CQDs exhibited uniform size, good up-conversion photoluminescence, excellent photostability, and moderate QYs (3.3–4.7 %). These properties suggest their suitability for bioimaging and optoelectronic applications (Fig. 8B).

While this method addresses several limitations of previous techniques—such as the poor size control and structural heterogeneity seen in combustion and microwave methods—it is not without drawbacks. The removal of the template often requires aggressive etching agents, which may leave residual impurities or introduce additional defects. Additionally, the process is relatively complex, time-consuming, and costly, and typically yields lower quantities of CQDs compared with simpler methods. Therefore, although template-assisted synthesis excels in precision and customization, it may not be ideal for large-scale or cost-sensitive applications. Future research may focus on combining the precision of templating with the scalability of simpler methods to optimize both performance and practicality.

The emergence of dynamic models that integrate diverse scientific disciplines—including physics, biology, energy storage, electronics, and environmental remediation—reflects a significant trend in research (Ahmed and Soylak, 2024). These modern approaches utilize the advanced capabilities of nanosystems to develop innovative zero-dimensional nanobiomaterials based on CQDs for medical applications (Ezoji and Rahimnejad, 2023). Since the application of CQDs principles and techniques in nanomedicine has revolutionized the medical industry and improved human health and well-being, this signifies a significant paradigm shift in healthcare (Das et al., 2024; Kumar et al., 2020). The key roles and responsibilities of nanomedicine include the application of medical physics techniques for the diagnosis, drug delivery, tissue engineering, treatment of human diseases, and providing a favorable environment for chemical agents (Kumar et al., 2020).

Recent advances in screening, targeting and immunotherapies have led to the development of theranostics platforms, as diagnostic therapeutics (Choi et al., 2012). These concepts - integration of imaging and therapy - are continuously under development by incorporating CQDs with other nanoparticles and biologically active molecules (Patel et al., 2019; Naik et al., 2022). A theranostics nanoplatform of eco-friendly trichrome-tryptophan-sorbitol CQDs, currently under investigation, is designed to synthesize substances that have a higher affinity for attacking hepatocellular carcinoma cells, leading to autophagy and halting tumor growth (Wang et al., 2022). Several studies have shown how CQDs are highly adaptable in biomedicine: (i) their customizable surface allows for different bioimaging methods and compatibility with various imaging agents, (ii) they are suitable for biosensors due to their reactions to different stimulation, and (iii) their ability to interact with biomolecules or drugs via various responses and stimuli makes them effective for delivering substances (Wang et al., 2014; Chai et al., 2022).( Table 1)

Modifying CQDs can be achieved by chemical (such as surface functionalization/passivation, doping, etc.) and physical (such as core-shell architecture, composite material blending, etc.) methods to regulate their characteristics (John et al., 2021). Table 2 showcases an extensive review of literature, overall categorization of CQDs modification techniques, and outlines notable uses of these materials in the field of biomedicine. It demonstrates how CQDs improve therapeutic efficacy and safety in various cancer therapies, with particular examples of research demonstrating their usefulness in clinical settings.

Molecular imaging involves observing, identifying, and quantifying biological functions at the molecular and cellular levels. As a swiftly advancing area in biomedicine, it utilizes sophisticated methods like fluorescent imaging, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and radionuclide imaging to observe in vivo molecular and cellular processes, along with disease mechanisms, in living beings (Matea et al., 2017; MacRitchie et al., 2020). Among the instruments propelling advancement in this area, quantum dots have surfaced as potent nanoprobes because of their adjustable optical characteristics and remarkable brightness. Nevertheless, traditional semiconductor quantum dots, like cadmium selenide/zinc sulfide (CdSe/ZnS) nanoparticles, encounter challenges in clinical application because of toxicity issues related to heavy metal elements (e.g., cadmium) and their instability with extended light exposure. Conversely, CQDs provide unique benefits as biocompatible options. In contrast to CdSe/ZnS QDs, CQDs consist of non-toxic carbon materials, reducing the dangers of cytotoxicity and environmental damage (Li et al., 2012; Mimona et al., 2025). Moreover, they show enhanced photostability, resisting photobleaching in prolonged imaging, and display remarkable chemical stability under various physiological conditions (Wang et al., 2014). These characteristics make CQDs strong contenders for secure, high-contrast molecular imaging in vivo, especially for uses that demand continuous or extended monitoring of cellular activities (Zheng et al., 2015).

Molecular imaging is a developing field that uses different imaging methods like fluorescent imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, and radionuclide imaging to capture in vivo mechanisms of molecular and cellular pathways, as well as diseases, inside living organisms (Deng et al., 2019). Among various types of nanoplatforms, carbon nanomaterials have shown significant promise for use in fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging applications.

Controlling and adjusting the size, shape, morphology, and surface modifications of carbon nanomaterials is essential for achieving biocompatibility, stability, and precise targeting in vivo, enabling biosensing and bioimaging applications for both diagnosis and therapy (Janus et al., 2019). Recent research suggests that carbon nanomaterials demonstrate promising potential for various biological applications (Bodunde et al., 2021). Bioimaging is a powerful non-invasive medical imaging technology that employs a strong and static magnetic field, magnetic gradients, and computer-induced radio waves to generate high-quality three-dimensional images of tissues and organs (Das et al., 2024).

Luminescent bioimaging techniques, employed for mapping molecular events within tissues, help realign cellular activities within this imaging field. The phenomenon of luminescence, which was first identified in 1888 by Eilhard Wiedemann, is characterized by various energy conversions occurring in molecules (Jessy Mercy et al., 2024). Consequently, the spectral properties of fluorophores enable high QYs, and fluorescent sensors are utilized to detect energy (signal) released from nanoprobes (Niu et al., 2014). Graphene quantum dots, a form of carbon dots, combine the features of carbon dots with the exceptional properties of graphene. Lately, GQDs have been widely used in various areas such as biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and other applications.

Fullerenes, carbon nanohorns (CNHs), and carbon nanoonions (CNOs), which are different types of carbon nanomaterials, possess distinct features that make them valuable for fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging (Wen et al., 2015). These CQDs derived from biopolymers have attracted significant interest because of their biodegradability, compatibility with biological systems, and non-toxic nature (Ken et al., 2018). Carbon dots have displayed significant potential in optical bioimaging, showcasing efficient absorption by cells and remarkable fluorescence intensity in cell conditions. The CQDs produce fluorescence when used in near-infrared (NIR) imaging (wavelength of 700–900 nm) (Tao et al., 2012). Perfect for bioimaging because of tissue transparency in the range of 400–800 nm, enhanced signal-to-noise ratio, and increased tissue penetration with longer wavelengths (Ge et al., 2015). Carbon dots have shown good performance in in vivo imaging with mouse models when compared with commercial semiconductor quantum dots such as CdSe-ZnS. Furthermore, evaluations of toxicity show that carbon dots are safe, with no toxicity in terms of cell or in vivo effects (Sun et al., 2006; Zhao et al., 2008; Yang et al., 2009a).

Fluorescence images demonstrate that CQDs are more luminous, do not flicker, and have a smaller size compared with CdSe/ZnS QDs, improving optical characteristics and abilities (Molaei, 2019). Research using CQDs for bioimaging was first carried out in 2009, with experiments done in vitro and in vivo (Yang et al., 2009a, 2009b). Afterwards, multiple CQDs were created through environmentally-friendly methods and demonstrated success in bioimaging uses. Different types of cells and tissues like bacteria (Das et al., 2017), yeast (Mehta et al., 2014), and fish (Jiang et al., 2014), as well as cancer cells and mice, were employed in imaging studies. There have also been numerous studies conducted on pinpointing substances within the cell (Shen et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2017). The CQDs extracted from Ginkgo leaves were successfully utilized for bioimaging HeLa cells. This application emphasizes their ability to monitor cellular processes and visualize cellular structures, which is crucial for research in cancer biology and other areas (Zhang et al., 2019).

The CQDs have emerged as groundbreaking tools in biosensing due to their unique physicochemical properties and versatile functionalization capabilities. As schematically illustrated in Fig. 9, their sensing mechanisms are based on target-induced modulation of optical or electronic signals, driven by nanoscale interactions. For instance, CQDs functionalized with specific surface groups (e.g., –NH₂, –COOH) selectively bind to analytes such as metal ions, proteins, or DNA, triggering detectable changes in fluorescence intensity through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or photoinduced electron transfer (PET) (Li et al., 2012). A notable example involves amine-functionalized CQDs for Hg²⁺ detection, where Hg²⁺ binding induces fluorescence quenching via PET, achieving a detection limit of 0.1 nM (Wang and Hu, 2014; Torabfam et al., 2022). Similarly, antibody-conjugated CQDs enable label-free detection of cancer biomarkers, such as prostate-specific antigen (PSA), by exploiting antigen-antibody binding to induce FRET-based signal changes (Fig. 9) (Matea et al., 2017).

The incorporation of CQDs into nanobioelectronic interfaces facilitates enhanced signal transduction efficiency in biomolecular sensing platforms. As demonstrated in CQDs-modified electrodes (Ferreira et al., 2023), these systems exhibit accelerated interfacial electron transfer kinetics, enabling ultrasensitive and selective real-time detection of critical biomarkers such as glucose and DNA.

Biosensors are widely used in various applications within the medical field. Quantum dots have been studied for biosensing purposes and have earned Nobel Prizes in 2023 (Jessy Mercy et al., 2024). Using nanotechnology for creating biosensors takes advantage of its benefits, emphasizing its crucial position in this field. The distinctive properties of nanomaterials can improve both the sensitivity and efficiency of biosensors, while also facilitating the creation of novel signal transduction technologies (Fraceto et al., 2016). Moreover, nanomaterials have allowed for the decrease in size of many sensors, such as (bio)sensors, resulting in the creation of small and intelligent devices like nanosensors and other nanosystems, which are essential in biochemical analysis (Sertova, 2015).

The tunable photoluminescence and inherent biocompatibility of CQDs render them highly suitable for multiplexed biosensing applications. Their intrinsic dual functionality—serving as both fluorescent emitters and FRET quenchers—highlights their multifunctionality in FRET-based systems, positioning CQDs as pivotal components for next-generation point-of-care (POC) diagnostic platforms (Dkhar et al., 2024).

A variety of nano-detection technology sensors, such as biosensors, electrochemical sensors, optical sensors, and devices, will be crucial for identifying small concentrations of heavy metals (Prasad et al., 2017). A biosensor gathers quantitative analytical information on a detector by utilizing a receptor located close to a transducer. Classification is done according to transduction and biological mechanisms. There are various types of biosensors, including optical, electrochemical, electronic, and piezoelectric sensors (Jessy Mercy et al., 2024).

Detection methods are primarily categorized into two types: label-free and labeled. In the label-free method, analytes interact directly with the transducer without requiring any additional components, while the labeled method involves a signal-producing label that binds to the analyte, facilitating detection. Optical biosensors utilize different sensor types to directly detect analytes. Surface Plasmon Resonance, which was first identified in 1902, provides benefits across various uses (Damborský et al., 2016; Jing et al., 2019).

A nanocomposite was developed by using CQDs derived from xylan to create a sensor capable of detecting dopamine. The sensor showed excellent sensitivity and specificity in electrochemical detection, utilizing silver nanoparticles and reduced graphene oxide (Han et al., 2021). Cho et al. created a glucose detection system with CQDs and rhodamine 6 G, emloying a ratiometric method. A change in color from blue to green was observed with an increase in glucose concentration. Their system had a wide linear range of 0.1–500 μM and a detection limit of 40 nM (Cho and Park, 2019). The electrochemical biosensor showed two different linear ranges, one in the µM range and the other in the millimolar range, with a minimum detectable limit of 530 nanometers. Wang and colleagues created a new system that enhanced immobilization methods by utilizing CoFe as a positively charged base to aid in horseradish peroxidase attachment and establish a direct electrical link with CQDs (Wang et al., 2015).

Considerable research has been conducted on the utilization of biosensors for detecting analytes and pathogens in various methods (Yan et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2023). A novel DNA biosensor probe was developed using CQDs derived from palm kernel shells and compared with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) synthesized via citrate reduction. The potential of this new DNA biosensor, which utilizes CQDs as fluorophores and AuNPs, is high in detecting the dengue (DEN-3) virus with sensitivity and specificity (Monday et al., 2023). The presence of CQDs caused an increase in direct electron transfer (DET), which was not lacking in the other control groups. Zhao and his team conducted research on using CQDs to enhance DET with both glucose oxidase and bilirubin oxidase (Zhao et al., 2015). Continuous studies are currently taking place in order to improve the characteristics of GQDs and CQDs by making chemical modifications, with the goal of broadening their potential uses (Cui et al., 2024; Subramania, 2023). They are commonly acknowledged for their application as biocompatible semiconductor quantum dots, providing possibilities to enhance luminescent characteristics and conduct real-time biological sensing (Mondal et al., 2023).

Challenges remain in controlling surface chemical compositions and electronic states to achieve optimal performance levels. GQDs and CQDs show great promise in improving biosensors due to their strong binding to biological components and efficiency in challenging environments. Their proficiency in technical abilities exceeds that of other nanosensing technologies, particularly in monitoring actions occurring inside living organisms. Advancements in the fields of chemistry, physics, and engineering are progressing to overcome challenges, indicating a bright future with the potential for increased biosensing (Suvarnaphaet and Pechprasarn, 2017; Chen et al., 2018).

The scientific community is highly interested in exploring the potential toxicity of various types of QDs, such as metal-free or CQDs, in order to gain a deeper understanding of the interactions between QDs and biological macromolecules (Matea et al., 2017). Research is being conducted on the potential uses of CQDs in medical delivery, specifically as sensors, and antimicrobial, neurodegenerative, and antineoplastic drugs. The potential benefits of CQDs in enhancing drug delivery and transporting pathogenic drugs to specific cells are also being investigated to further validate their effectiveness as carriers of medications (Krishnaswamy and Pandian, 2022; Raeispour et al., 2024).

The CQDs are currently being used for precise drug delivery in cancer treatment (Su et al., 2020) and in the field of nanomedicine (Bayda et al., 2021). These CQDs are employed in drug delivery for accurate and soluble drug release, allowing for controlled monitoring of targeted release through covalent or non-covalent links (Naik et al., 2022). It is estimated that by 2040, there will be 27.5 million new cases of cancer and 16.2 million deaths caused solely by the disease (Ferlay et al., 2019). In this context, nanomedicine represents an innovative approach in the medical field that can play a significant role.

Various nanosystems currently under research for accurate drug administration in cancer therapy aim to enhance the efficacy of drugs like mitoxantrone (MTX), which disrupts the functions of cancer cells (Dias-Carvalho et al., 2023). The MTX medication has shown to be a very successful therapy for different types of multiple sclerosis, like relapsing-remitting, progressive-relapsing, and secondary progressive, by slowing down the advancement of neurological disability (El-Emam et al., 2022). Using a hydrothermal method, a novel nanocarrier combining Nitrogen-doped CQDs and MTX has displayed encouraging outcomes in improving treatment efficacy. The CQDs have shown promise in forming complexes with high drug loading efficiency and enhancing electrostatic interactions with MTX.

Research on drug delivery shows that these CQDs-MTX complexes have higher drug release in acidic conditions, which is advantageous for targeting tumors. Additionally, tests conducted with the MTT assay show notable suppression of cancer cell proliferation when cells are exposed to CQDs-MTX compounds. Moreover, CQDs are considered as possible drug delivery options due to their antioxidant characteristics (Raeispour et al., 2024). Recent studies emphasize the capacity of CQDs to serve as photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy (PDT) and as nanocarriers for drug delivery. Their suitability for living organisms, combined with their improved characteristics, makes them perfect contenders for cancer therapy. Nevertheless, there are still difficulties in the production, development, and medical use of CQDs.

In spite of these challenges, current researches offer hope for their potentials in cancer treatment (Soumya et al., 2023). Another study by Chiu et al., (2016) developed a nanotheranostic platform by merging CQDs with tri-elements: Gadolinium (Gd), Nitrogen (N), and Sulfur (S), resulting in the formation of GdNS@CQDs. Folic acid (FA) was integrated into the GdNS@CQDs through a 1-Ethyl-3-(3- dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide ECD/Sulfo-NHS reaction to enable combined fluorescence and MRI targeting. This nanoplatform was subsequently tested for targeting efficiency on human cervical cancer (HeLa) and human liver cancer (HepG2) cells. The resulting nanoplatforms exhibited notable stability, biocompatibility, and minimal toxicity. Furthermore, the FA-GdNS@CQDs demonstrated an 80 % capacity to transport the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX), with its release influenced by pH levels (Su et al., 2020). Therefore, these nano- theranostics systems are known for their ability to provide dual-contrast imaging (fluorescence/magnetic resonance), as well as the capability to pinpoint cancer cells and administer drugs in a pH-sensitive manner. The CQDs containing doxorubicin have demonstrated a strong ability to destroy both cancer cells and cancer stem cells (Su et al., 2020).

Curcumin has strong anti-cancer properties, but its use in medicine is hindered by its low solubility (Tabanelli et al., 2021). In order to address this obstacle, scientists have merged chitosan, alumina, and CQDs to create a highly effective nanocarrier for delivering curcumin. It showed stability, high drug loading, controlled release, and promising results in cancer treatment (Karami et al., 2023). Improvements can also be achieved by decreasing the negative side effects and presence of the drug in the body while extending its expiration date. Overall, the recommended nanocarrier exhibits favorable attributes as a precise, controlled, and pH-responsive approach for consistently administering curcumin as an anti-cancer agent (Karami et al., 2023).

The CQDs play a role in drug transport and visualization, yet encounter problems with compatibility in living organisms and the process of creating them can be difficult (Das et al., 2024). In general, the integration of CQDs with drug conjugates shows substantial promise in improving focused transportation. This benefit comes from CQDs' capability to provide improved management of drug release targeting, which can be accomplished through methods like pH alterations or exposure to light (Naik et al., 2022).

In cancer diagnostics, CQDs facilitate ultrasensitive detection of tumor-derived biomarkers via integration into miniaturized, micro/nanoscale cytometric platforms (Purohit et al., 2019). Rational surface engineering of CQDs with tumor-targeting ligands (e.g., monoclonal antibodies) enables precise molecular recognition of cancer cell surface receptors, significantly enhancing diagnostic specificity.
