Moreover, photothermally active CQDs architectures (Debnath et al., 2024) exhibit dual-modal theranostic functionality, synergizing real-time imaging (e.g., NIR fluorescence/photoacoustic) with spatially controlled therapeutic interventions such as localized photothermal ablation. This convergence of diagnostics and therapy underscores the transformative potential of CQDs in advancing precision nanotheranostics.

Different methods, such as the use of citric acid and urea, are employed to synthesize CQDs, which can be conjugated with drugs like DOX (Zeng et al., 2016). Additionally, phosphorus and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (PNHCD) have been created. These PNHCD, with a notable size of 2.05 nm, are synthesized from the plant Nerium oleander L., while CQDs are produced from E. coli, and these nanodots can incorporate compounds like Rhodamine 6 G and DOX for enhanced therapeutic efficacy (Gong et al., 2016). Furthermore, researchers Jha et al. (2020) and Yadav et al. (2021) have developed carbon dots based on DNA and proteins for cancer treatment, emphasizing the versatility of CQDs in drug delivery. In another approach, Ma et al., (2018) and Yunus et al., (2020) utilized ultrasonication techniques to derive CQDs from glucose and sucrose, highlighting an innovative method for producing CQDs aimed at improving drug delivery in chemotherapy applications. Fig. 10 presents a detailed overview of the clinical applications of CQDs, highlighting their use in areas such as targeted drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, imaging techniques, and biosensing.

Gene therapy is an innovative medical technology designed to treat genetic disorders, cancers, and viral infections. However, challenges related to safety and ethics continue to arise. Nanotechnology plays a vital role in enhancing the precision and efficiency of these therapies. CQDs show great promise in gene delivery (Othman et al., 2024). The primary goal of gene therapy is to introduce foreign DNA into the body to treat diseases. Due to their biocompatibility and high transfection efficiency, CQDs can serve as effective gene vectors, facilitating the creation of complexes for delivering small interfering RNA (siRNA) and for applications in chondrogenesis (Molaei, 2019). In recent years, Folic acid (FA), hyaluronic acid (HA), and arginine–glycine–aspartic acid (RGD-peptide) have all been used as fluorescent probes in conjunction with CDs for disease diagnosis and therapy (Biswal and Bhatia, 2021).

Progress in gene therapy has been made across various diseases as the development of new vectors leads to safer and more efficient treatment options (Mondal et al., 2023). The CQDs are able to deliver genes both in vitro and in vivo, and can be easily tracked for imaging purposes (Liu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2014). In 2012, Liu and colleagues produced (CD-PEI) that was functionalized with polyethyleneimine (PEI) as cationic polymers by using microwave assistance to pyrolyze glycerol and branched PEI25k. The CD-PEIs displayed noticeable fluorescence features for imaging living organisms and effective abilities to deliver DNA, varying in compositions and sizes. They were effective in condensing DNA for cellular transport and could dissolve in water. Wang et al., (2014) developed novel Alkyl-PEI2k-CDots for gene delivery by utilizing Alkyl-PEI2k for passivation.

These nanovectors demonstrated consistency, durability, and brightness. Alkyl-PEI2k-CDots exhibited strong attachment, effectively protecting and facilitating the transmission of small interfering RNA (siRNA) and plasmid DNA (pDNA) while causing minimal harm to cells. In a related advancement, CQDs have also been conjugated with hyaluronic acid to enhance the delivery of doxorubicin, leading to significant improvements in cancer treatment (Wang et al., 2022). The Arginine-CQDs carboplex shows superior gene transfer capability with lower toxicity when compared with the PEI polyplex, which is considered the "gold standard". Furthermore, this cargo shows great stability over long periods in physiological media. The remarkable outcomes and stable fluorescence of CQDs enhance their potential for real-time tracking and controlled delivery (Rezaei, Hashemi, 2021).

Alaghmandfard et al., (2021) discovered that CQDs can effectively deliver genes by combining them with polyamidoamine to target triple-negative breast cancer. In gene therapy, carbon nanostructures improve drug delivery and treatment efficacy. They interact with biological systems, transforming clinical practices through innovative methods. Researchers worldwide are focusing on non-viral nanostructures to develop advanced gene therapies. These innovations have the potential to replace expensive therapies with more cost-effective alternatives for various diseases. Further research is needed in gene therapy, particularly involving nanostructure-based systems like carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and (QDs), to enhance delivery efficiency (Yazdani et al., 2024).

Among the available treatment options, photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) have emerged as prominent choices that utilize light. Historically, psoralens were used in conjunction with light to treat vitiligo in ancient Egypt and India (Celli et al., 2010). Niels Finsen was awarded the Nobel Prize in 1903 for his pioneering work in modern phototherapy (Xie et al., 2020). Photo-induced cancer treatment uses PDT and PTT processes to cause localized cell death in tumor tissues. PDT creates ROS using a photosensitizer under certain light conditions, whereas PTT generates heat with a photothermal agent.

Combining the two strategies enhances therapy by employing light energy for reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and hyperthermia. This collaboration offers complete cancer therapy and imaging applications, which improve therapeutic results (Jing et al., 2024). Recent studies indicate that the combination of PDT and PTT offers significant advantages in tumor destruction and drug delivery, particularly in cases of brain cancer (Nasseri et al., 2022; Di et al., 2020). Additionally, effective antimicrobial photodynamic inactivation nanoparticles can be produced through a simple manufacturing process, enhancing their use for antimicrobial purposes (Xing et al., 2019).

Nie et al. (2020) developed antibacterial CQDs-embedded polyacrylonitrile nanofibers (PAN-CQDs NFs) using an electrospinning technique, with the CQDs synthesized from citric acid and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene. Photodynamic inactivation of Gram-negative bacteria is generally considered more challenging than that of Gram-positive bacteria due to the presence of an outer cell membrane. However, observations indicate that bactericidal efficiency can effectively inactivate Gram-negative bacteria through photodynamic treatment, suggesting the potential of Type II photodynamic materials. Moreover, poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA)-based nanoparticles are being explored to address antibiotic resistance in bacterial biofilms. The combination of CQDs with PLGA nanoparticles enhances drug loading and release control (Huang et al., 2020).

According to Yoon et al. (2017), Camellia japonica L., a plant rich in bioactive chemicals, offers a number of advantages with an anti-hyperuricemic effect. To improve photothermal treatment, Kim et al., (2021) synthesized sulfur-doped CQDs (S-CQDs) from C. Japonica blossoms. With a photothermal conversion efficiency of 55.4 %, these CQDs exhibit effective PTT action both in vitro and in vivo. In addition, nanostructures derived from Ayurvedic herbs, exhibited great potential for use in medical imaging and therapy. The CQDs-1 (Azadirachta indica A.Juss.), CQDs-2 (Ocimum tenuiflorum L.), and CQDs-3 (Tridax procumbens L.) that were isolated from the leaves of ayurvedic plants showed promise for phototherapy and cancer cell imaging. They also emitted green fluorescence, had a uniform size distribution of about 6–12 nm, average photo-stability, and over 85 % biocompatibility (Meena et al., 2019).

Challenges in cancer therapy often arise from the complex tumor microenvironment, atypical proliferation rates, and resistance to medication. PDT offers several advantages, including ease of use, safety, and minimal side effects. The development of PDT has been significantly advanced by the use of nanocarriers, which help overcome the limitations of traditional photosensitizers while also improving imaging and surgical guidance. Combination treatments that incorporate PDT show promise in addressing treatment constraints and enhancing effectiveness in tumor management (Mroz and Hamblin, 2011). PDT may cause long-lasting overall immune system suppression, potentially due to the deactivation of damage-associated molecular patterns by reactive oxygen species and the secretion of immunosuppressive proteins by cancer cells. This immune suppression can adversely affect treatment outcomes and limit the potential for combining PDT with immunotherapy, PTT, and radiotherapy. Additionally, challenges related to nanocarrier safety evaluations hinder the effective use of multidrug delivery systems in clinical settings (Yang et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs application in environmental and agricultural systems
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots are nanoscale carbon-based materials that possess unique optical and electronic properties, combined with low environmental toxicity and excellent biocompatibility due to their non-toxic carbon framework. These attributes make CQDs highly promising candidates for agricultural applications ( Fig. 11). Recent studies highlight their potential in addressing key challenges in modern agriculture. CQDs have been explored for their ability to enhance crop productivity, improve the efficiency of fertilizers and pesticides, and support the management of plant diseases and environmental stresses. Additionally, they offer capabilities for real-time monitoring of crop health (Li et al., 2020). This section outlines the diverse agricultural applications of CQDs.

Carbon quantum dots have emerged as promising bio-stimulants for enhancing plant growth. However, the physiological mechanisms underlying their effects on crop development are still not fully understood, and research in this area remains at an early stage (Li et al., 2023). A notable breakthrough was reported by Qu et al. (2012), who demonstrated the biocompatibility of CQDs with bean sprouts, suggesting their safe application in agriculture without adverse effects on plant health. More recent studies have confirmed the growth-promoting effects of CQDs across a diverse range of crops, including wheat, rice, maize, mung beans, tomatoes, lettuce, tobacco, and soybeans (Li et al., 2024). Several mechanisms have been proposed to explain these effects. CQDs may enhance the bioavailability of essential nutrients by improving their solubility and facilitating their uptake and transport within plant tissues.

Recent studies have underscored the role of CQDs in improving water and nutrient uptake in plants. The surface of CQDs is rich in hydrophilic functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which provide multiple binding sites for water molecules. This characteristic enhances water retention and increases moisture availability within plant seeds (Wang et al., 2018b). In addition, the inherent adsorptive properties of CQDs allow them to bind essential metal ions—such as iron, zinc, and manganese—that are critical for plant development, facilitating their transport and bioavailability in plant tissues (Li et al., 2020).

In addition to their effects on plant physiology, the potential long-term impact of CQDs on soil microbiota is an important consideration for their sustainable agricultural application. Soil microbial communities play a crucial role in nutrient cycling, organic matter decomposition, and overall soil fertility. Recent studies have shown that while low concentrations of CQDs may have negligible or even beneficial effects on microbial activity, higher doses or prolonged exposure can alter microbial diversity and functionality (Zuo et al., 2024; Prajapati et al., 2025). For instance, some carbon-based nanomaterials have been found to inhibit key microbial taxa involved in nitrogen fixation or phosphorus solubilization (Khan et al., 2022). Therefore, more in-depth and long-term studies are needed to fully understand the ecological footprint of CQDs in agricultural soils and ensure that their use does not disrupt the microbial balance critical to soil health.

Due to photoluminescent properties, CQDs can serve as fluorescent markers for imaging plant tissues and tracking plant growth and health, helping researchers understand growth patterns and nutrient distribution (Alam et al., 2015; Tan et al., 2023). Techniques such as confocal laser scanning microscopy are employed to visualize and confirm the presence of CQDs within plant tissues (Shukla et al., 2020).

The plant cell wall, composed of primary and secondary layers rich in pectin, hemicellulose, cellulose, and lignin, possesses nanoscale pore sizes that allow the penetration of CQDs (Tan et al., 2023). Once inside, CQDs can associate with cell wall components through covalent and ionic bonds, enabling their translocation via apoplastic and symplastic pathways influenced by transpiration pull and diffusion coefficients (Zarebanadkouki et al., 2019; Ali et al., 2021). Physiologically, CQDs contribute to improved seed germination rate, root development, and overall plant vigor. Two primary methods are employed for their application: seed soaking and foliar spraying. In seed soaking—also referred to as seed priming—seeds are immersed in a CQDs solution for a specified duration prior to planting, promoting efficient uptake through lateral roots, root hairs, and root tips (Abdel-Aziz et al., 2019).

Foliar application involves spraying a CQDs solution directly onto plant leaves, allowing the nanomaterials to enter through stomata, cuticles, wounds, and hydathodes, typically starting from the seedling stage (Ali et al., 2021). Although the drenching method, which targets the root zone, can facilitate deeper penetration into the root system, it is generally slower than foliar application. In contrast, foliar application enables rapid, precise, and localized delivery of CQDs to photosynthetically active tissues. Comparative studies have demonstrated that foliar application is more efficient for introducing CQDs into plants. Both methods, however, have been shown to significantly enhance root and shoot length, overall biomass, photosynthetic activity, and the expression of growth-related genes in CQDs-treated plants (Tan et al., 2021).

The CQDs can also improve chlorophyll content and photosynthetic efficiency, leading to increased biomass and yield. The CQDs are shown to improve photosynthesis through their unique light absorption properties, particularly in the ultraviolet and visible light ranges (Li et al., 2018b; Ge et al., 2024). They serve as effective electron donors and acceptors, facilitating photon absorption and electron transfer within chloroplasts, thereby accelerating the photosynthetic electron transport chain (Molaei, 2020). Notably, CQDs enhance chlorophyll content, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) synthase activity, and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) synthesis, leading to a significant increase in the electron transfer chain (Chandra et al., 2014). The expression of chlorophyll synthase and related genes is also elevated, promoting chlorophyll synthesis and CO2 assimilation (Li et al., 2021). Furthermore, CQDs increase Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase (RuBisCo) enzyme activity in rice (Li et al., 2018a), Arabidopsis thaliana (L.) Heynh. (Li et al., 2019), and mung bean (Wang et al., 2018b) seedlings. The impact of CQDs on photosynthesis is more pronounced in monocotyledonous plants compared with dicotyledonous ones, attributed to structural differences (Maholiya et al., 2023). The findings demonstrate that CQDs facilitate artificial photosynthesis support for crops, leading to increased photosynthetic rates and, consequently, higher grain yields. Key morphological and physiological traits are all positively influenced by the application of CQDs (Tan et al., 2023).

Despite the established advantages of CQDs, optimal concentrations for enhancing plant growth vary due to differences in synthesis methods and precursors, necessitating further research to determine the ideal dosage for various crops (Chu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2024). While some studies suggest that higher concentrations of CQDs may promote photosynthesis, it is crucial to investigate potential cytotoxicity and genotoxicity to ensure consumer safety (Guo et al., 2022). Current knowledge gaps include the electron transfer mechanisms of CQDs in different plant types, the longevity of CQDs activity post-application, and the correlation between physiological effects and photosynthetic performance metrics (Tan et al., 2023). Additionally, the impact of CQDs on chlorophyll fluorescence parameters and QY remains underexplored. Future research should also focus on the role of CQDs in indoor farming, particularly regarding the effects of varying light spectra, intensities, and durations on plant growth.

The CQDs represent a promising development in agricultural biotechnology, offering significant potential as biostimulants to enhance plant growth and development. Their multifaceted mechanisms of action underscore their role in optimizing crop yield. The ability of CQDs to enhance plant growth through various application methods highlights their versatility in agricultural practices. As studies continue to explore the physiological implications of CQDs in agriculture, their integration into agricultural practices could lead to improved crop yield and sustainability. Ultimately, the ongoing research into CQDs holds the potential to revolutionize crop management strategies, contributing to food security and environmental resilience in the face of global challenges.

Carbon quantum dots have been found to be effective in plant resistance to biotic and abiotic stresses. They may help plants withstand abiotic stresses such as drought, salinity, and extreme temperatures by modulating stress-responsive genes. The CQDs have radical scavenging properties due to the presence of electron-donating and electron-withdrawing carboxyl and amino acid groups, and by the mechanism of transferring a hydrogen atom by CQDs, unstable and active radicals are converted into stable forms (Foyer and Noctor, 2005; Li et al., 2020). Studies have shown that CQDs increase the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), and catalase (CAT) in peanuts and reduce their malondialdehyde (MAD) content, which is an indicator of oxidative stress. As a result, CQDs reduce drought stress in plants by increasing stress resistance (Su et al., 2018). The CQDs by combining the radical scavenging property and also expanding antioxidant enzyme activity, can protect plants from abiotic stresses.

Furthermore, the protective effects of CQDs against abiotic stress have been observed in various plant species as well (Dong et al., 2022; Panahirad et al., 2023; Farhangi-Abriz et al., 2024). The CQDs act as antioxidants, shielding the plants from UV radiation damage and promoting growth and biomass accumulation (Zhang et al., 2018).

The treatment with CQDs could play a significant role in improving the disease resistance of crops. This enhancement is achieved by stimulating the over-expression of genes that are associated with plant defense mechanisms against diseases (Li et al., 2018a).

Traditional use of bactericides and fungicides in agriculture poses risks to biodiversity and ecosystem health. The issue of controlling plant infectious and fungal agents is very important in agriculture, and the antibacterial and antifungal mechanism of CQDs can be a great help in controlling plant pathogens, as these particles cause the destruction of the bacterial wall and also cause their death by damaging the genetic content of bacteria and fungi (Li et al., 2020). While CQDs have not been widely applied in agricultural disease management, their broad-spectrum antibacterial and antifungal properties have been documented (Wu et al., 2021; Safardoust-Hojaghan et al., 2021; Ezati et al., 2022). The selective antibacterial activity of CQDs against anaerobic microorganisms has been previously reported (Li et al., 2017).

The CQDs exhibit antibacterial properties primarily by damaging the cell walls of bacteria. This damage is further enhanced by the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are highly reactive molecules that can lead to oxidative stress in microbial cells.

The CQDs can be applied to detect specific pathogens in plants, enabling early intervention (Wang et al., 2021). In addition, CQDs can stimulate the secretion of root exudates such as organic and fatty acids thereby regulate the beneficial soil microorganisms including Proteobacteria, Acidobacteria, and Actinobacteria and promote a healthy soil microbiome in, which is crucial for nutrient cycling and plant health (Wang et al., 2022a).

The researches demonstrate that CQDs enhance nitrogenase activity by influencing its secondary structure and improving electron transfer in the biocatalytic process (Wang et al., 2018a; Ouyang et al., 2024). Therefore, the application of CQDs offer a sustainable solution to boost plant growth in nitrogen-deficient soils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of metal ions and other emerging pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of heavy metal ions in soil and water can severely impact agricultural crop health, yield, and safety. Heavy metals such as lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), arsenic (As), and mercury (Hg) can originate from various sources, including industrial runoff, fertilizers, and pesticides (Angon et al., 2024). The accumulation of these metals in agricultural products poses significant risks to human health and the environment. Thus, developing effective sensing technologies to monitor metal ion concentrations in agricultural settings is crucial (Li et al., 2024). The CQDs have emerged as promising materials for this purpose due to their unique properties.

Carbon quantum dots are also non-toxic and environmentally friendly, exhibit strong photoluminescence with size-tunable emission properties, allowing for the detection of specific metal ions based on changes in fluorescence intensity (Lu et al., 2022). The surface of CQDs can also be easily modified with various functional groups to enhance their selectivity and sensitivity toward specific metal ions. This functionalization can improve interaction with target analytes and increase detection efficiency (Yang et al., 2023).

The CQDs utilize several mechanisms to detect metal ions in agricultural environments. Many metal ions can quench the fluorescence of CQDs, with the extent of quenching correlating with the concentration of the metal ions (Salahinejad et al., 2021). This allows for quantitative analysis of metal ion levels in soil or water samples. Some metal ions can form stable complexes with functional groups on the surface of CQDs, leading to enhanced fluorescence (Shabbir et al., 2023). This property can be exploited to develop highly sensitive sensors for specific metal ions. The presence of certain metal ions can also induce aggregation of CQDs, resulting in changes in fluorescence properties that can be correlated with metal ion concentration (Raj et al., 2021). The interaction between CQDs and metal ions can alter local pH levels, affecting the fluorescence characteristics of CQDs (Das et al., 2023). This property can be utilized for detecting pH changes associated with metal ion presence.

Regular monitoring of soil quality is essential for sustainable agriculture. The CQDs can be used to develop sensors that detect heavy metal contamination in soil, enabling farmers to assess soil health and make informed decisions about crop cultivation and fertilization practices. In addition, CQDs-based sensors can be employed to monitor heavy metal concentrations in irrigation water sources, ensuring that crops are not exposed to harmful levels of contaminants (Wang et al., 2022b). Interestingly, CQDs can be used to develop rapid screening methods for detecting metal ion residues in harvested crops, ensuring food safety and compliance with regulatory standards (Aksouh et al., 2024).

Compared with various nanomaterials used for metal ion detection, CQDs present several unique advantages in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and environmental compatibility. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are known for their strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR), which provides rapid and sensitive colorimetric detection of heavy metal ions. However, they typically require surface modification with thiol groups, aptamers, or DNA to achieve target selectivity (de Almeida et al., 2014; Prosposito et al., 2020). Traditional semiconductor quantum dots such as CdSe/ZnS offer high QYs and tunable fluorescence, but their heavy metal content raises toxicity concerns, limiting their application in agricultural environments (Banerjee and Santra, 2010). In contrast, CQDs are inherently biocompatible, photostable, and possess diverse surface functional groups that can interact directly with specific metal ions, enabling label-free and selective detection (Yang et al., 2023). Additionally, graphene-based nanocomposites exhibit excellent electrical conductivity and large surface area, which are advantageous for electrochemical sensing platforms, yet they often lack the intrinsic fluorescence needed for optical detection (Khalajiolyaie and Jian, 2025). Therefore, CQDs strike a balance between selectivity, safety, and versatility, making them promising candidates for sustainable and field-deployable sensing technologies in agriculture.

Despite the advantages, several challenges remain in the application of CQDs for sensing metal ions in agriculture (Dong et al., 2024). Developing reliable calibration methods for CQDs-based sensors in complex agricultural matrices is essential for accurate field applications. The presence of other ions in soil or water can interfere with the detection of target metal ions. Therefore, research is needed to enhance the selectivity of CQDs-based sensors in complex environments (Devi et al., 2019). Ensuring the stability and longevity of CQDs sensors under varying environmental conditions is crucial for practical applications in agriculture (Ahmed and Soylak, 2024). Future research should focus on optimizing the synthesis and functionalization of CQDs, developing multi-analyte sensors capable of detecting multiple metal ions simultaneously, and integrating CQDs-based sensing technologies into smart agricultural practices (Castro et al., 2024; El-Azazy et al., 2024).

Carbon quantum dots hold significant promise for sensing metal ions in agriculture due to their unique properties, biocompatibility, and versatility. Their application in soil testing, water quality monitoring, food safety assessment, and precision agriculture can help mitigate the risks associated with heavy metal contamination, ultimately contributing to sustainable agricultural practices and food security.

Another current problem in agriculture is the increasing and excessive use of pesticides, herbicides, chemical fertilizers, and plant growth regulators, which can be controlled by CQDs (Li et al., 2024). The CQDs can be used in the development of nano-sensors for real-time monitoring of soil and plant health (Parameswari et al., 2024). The CQDs-based sensors can detect nutrient levels in the soil, allowing farmers to optimize fertilization practices (Hameed et al., 2023).

Conventional fertilizers, produced through energy-intensive methods like the Haber-Bosch process and mining, remain economically viable despite high production costs (Ojelade et al., 2023). In contrast, CQDs can be synthesized through scalable top-down and bottom-up methods, such as hydrothermal and solvothermal processes, offering a cost-effective alternative (Kaur et al., 2022; Rani et al., 2024a). Carbon quantum dots can act as carriers for fertilizers, increasing their release and uptake by plants. This slow-release mechanism reduces nutrient leaching and increases soil fertility (Alikhani et al., 2023). While CQDs is not designed to replace fertilizers, its application could enhance photosynthesis and crop yields, potentially reducing excessive fertilization and mitigating environmental impacts. Although the long-term environmental effects are not yet fully understood, it is expected that CQDs will eventually become inert in ecosystems (Rani et al., 2024b). Challenges remain in implementing CQDs on a large scale, particularly in precision farming, but advancements like hyperspectral imaging could facilitate real-time monitoring of crop health (Lu et al., 2020). Additionally, further research is needed to assess the nutritional composition of CQDs-treated crops compared with untreated ones to maximize the benefits of this innovative approach to sustainable agriculture (Tan et al., 2023).

Therefore, CQDs can be used in formulations to protect crops from diseases without the harmful effects associated with conventional pesticides. They can also be included into coatings or formulations to prevent pest damage while having less toxicity to beneficial insects and the environment. Application of CQDs as environmentally friendly alternatives aligns with sustainable agricultural practices. By enhancing nutrient efficiency and pest resistance, CQDs can also reduce the need for chemical inputs in agriculture.

The CQDs represent a promising class of photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials whose structure and emission characteristics are strongly influenced by the specific synthesis methods employed. These nanomaterials exhibit a range of exceptional properties, including strong photoluminescence, high chemical stability, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, enabling their use across diverse scientific fields. This review has provided a comprehensive overview of the structural and optical properties of CQDs, explored various synthesis methods and precursor types, and highlighted their broad applications in both biomedical and agricultural domains.

The CQDs possess a core–shell chemical structure that can be either graphitic crystalline (sp²) or amorphous (a mix of sp²/sp³ domains). They are typically synthesized using either top-down or bottom-up approaches. Bottom-up methods are generally favored due to their cost-effectiveness and environmental friendliness, although they offer limited control over particle size. In contrast, top-down techniques often require longer processing times and are associated with higher costs. Both green and chemical precursors have been widely utilized to produce highly fluorescent CQDs.

The fundamental properties of CQDs are closely linked to their structure, size, surface defects, and functional groups—factors that significantly influence their QY, optical bandgap, toxicity, solubility, biocompatibility, and stability. Their PL arises from various mechanisms, including emissive surface defects, heteroatom doping, surface chemical states, and the quantum confinement effect. This diverse emission behavior makes CQDs highly suitable for applications in bioimaging, catalysis, light-emitting devices, solar cells, supercapacitors, phototherapy, photocatalysis, and sensing technologies. Additionally, CQDs exhibit up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL), a phenomenon in which they emit higher-energy light than the excitation wavelength—an attractive feature for photocatalysis and cellular imaging. Moreover, doping and co-doping with various heteroatoms can effectively tailor the electronic structure of CQDs, thereby enhancing their optical and catalytic performance.

The convergence of multiple scientific disciplines has facilitated the development of advanced nanosystems for delivering innovative zero-dimensional nanobiomaterials—most notably CQDs—in the field of healthcare. The CQDs play a pivotal role in nanomedicine by improving diagnostics, enabling personalized drug delivery, and enhancing therapeutic outcomes, particularly in cancer treatment, owing to their excellent biocompatibility and potential for surface functionalization. In molecular imaging, carbon-based nanomaterials such as GQDs and CQDs have shown great efficacy in fluorescence-based bioimaging and biosensing applications.

Nanotechnology-enabled biosensors have significantly improved the sensitivity of analyte detection, and ongoing research continues to refine GQDs and CQDs for emerging clinical applications. Furthermore, CQDs contribute to gene therapy by facilitating efficient gene delivery, thereby advancing treatments for genetic disorders and cancers. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) have also emerged as promising cancer treatment strategies that rely on light energy to generate ROS or localized heat for tumor destruction—though comprehensive evaluation of their immunological effects is still required. In summary, CQDs represent a major breakthrough in diagnostic and therapeutic technologies. However, thorough investigations into their long-term safety and clinical efficacy are critical for successful medical translation.

The CQDs hold immense promise for revolutionizing both agricultural practices and biomedical technologies. In agriculture, the future likely involves developing highly efficient and targeted CQDs-based biosensors for precise monitoring of soil nutrients, plant health indicators, and pathogen detection. This will enable precision farming techniques, optimizing resource allocation and maximizing crop yields while minimizing environmental impact. Additionally, CQDs may be employed in advanced agricultural applications, such as controlled and site-specific delivery of pesticides or fertilizers, thereby minimizing waste and enhancing overall efficiency.

In the biomedical field, the future of CQDs appears exceptionally promising. Their unique optical and electronic properties make them ideal candidates for next-generation diagnostics and therapeutics. We anticipate further advancements in bioimaging, with CQDs offering enhanced resolution and improved sensitivity for early disease detection. Moreover, the development of biocompatible and targeted CQDs-based drug delivery systems will pave the way for personalized medicine, enabling precise treatment with minimal side effects. Ongoing research into photothermal therapy and other CQDs-based therapeutic strategies is also likely to yield major breakthroughs in the treatment of cancer and other complex diseases.

Despite their promising properties, several critical challenges must be addressed to fully translate CQDs from laboratory research to real-world applications. One of the main hurdles is scalability. While bottom-up synthesis techniques (e.g., hydrothermal, microwave-assisted) are cost-effective and environmentally benign, they often result in batch-to-batch variations and lack precise control over size distribution, surface passivation, and QY uniformity. These issues hinder the mass production of high-quality CQDs with reproducible physicochemical characteristics, which is essential for industrial and clinical applications.

Another significant concern lies in toxicity and biocompatibility. Although CQDs generally exhibit low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility in vitro, comprehensive long-term in vivo studies are scarce. Questions remain regarding their biodistribution, metabolism, potential accumulation in vital organs, and possible chronic toxicity or immunogenic responses. Additionally, the physicochemical variability between batches makes it difficult to predict biological responses consistently. This underlines the need for standardized toxicity assays, chronic exposure models, and real-time tracking approaches to ensure biosafety, especially for biomedical applications like drug delivery and imaging.

Furthermore, the regulatory landscape for nanomaterials, particularly zero-dimensional nanostructures like CQDs, remains underdeveloped. There is a lack of internationally harmonized standards for their characterization, toxicological profiling, and quality control. The translation of CQDs-based products into clinical practice faces regulatory bottlenecks due to insufficient guidelines on acceptable impurity levels, reproducibility, and pharmacokinetics. Regulatory authorities such as the FDA and EMA have not yet defined specific pathways for the approval of CQDs-based therapeutics, diagnostics, or agricultural nanoformulations, making market entry a considerable challenge.

Addressing these limitations will require coordinated interdisciplinary efforts that integrate materials science, toxicology, regulatory science, and application-specific engineering. Establishing standardized protocols for large-scale synthesis, thorough biosafety evaluations, and engagement with regulatory agencies will be essential for enabling the safe, reliable, and widespread implementation of CQDs in biomedicine and agriculture.

The application of CQDs in agriculture is a rapidly evolving field with significant potential to address challenges related to food security, environmental sustainability, and efficient resource use. Ongoing research is needed to fully understand their mechanisms of action, optimize their formulations, and evaluate their long-term effects on ecosystems. As technology advances, CQDs could play a crucial role in the future of sustainable agriculture practices.

Future directions should also consider commercial and regulatory aspects that may hinder the practical deployment of CQDs. Although numerous studies have demonstrated the potential of CQDs in both agriculture and biomedicine, translating these findings into market-ready technologies faces critical challenges. On the commercial side, issues such as large-scale, reproducible, and cost-effective production must be resolved, particularly with respect to maintaining consistent optical and structural properties. On the regulatory side, the absence of standardized evaluation protocols, particularly for toxicity, long-term environmental impact, and human safety, limits the clinical and agricultural adoption of CQDs-based products. Therefore, future research should not only focus on technological innovation but also prioritize developing regulatory frameworks and commercial strategies to ensure safe and scalable implementation of CQDs across different sectors.
